g.data-narration: Welcome to Event Handling in Swing. Learn how Java creates interactive GUI applications.
g.text: Understanding Event Handling in Java Swing

g.data-narration: Event handling responds to user actions like clicks and key presses, making GUIs interactive and responsive.
g.text: An event is an occurrence of a user action (click, key press, window close).
Event Handling is the mechanism that controls program flow in response to these events.
It is the cornerstone of creating interactive, responsive GUI applications.

g.data-narration: Swing uses the Delegation Event Model, separating the UI source from the listener logic for cleaner code.
g.text: Swing uses the Delegation Event Model - a modern, efficient approach.
It separates the UI component (source) from the behavior logic (listener).
This promotes cleaner, modular, maintainable code architecture.

g.data-narration: The model has three key participants: Event Source fires events, Event Listener processes them, and Event Object holds data.
Events flow from user action through the source creating an event object, which is then sent to registered listeners.
Java provides event classes like ActionEvent for buttons, MouseEvent for mouse actions, and KeyEvent for keyboard input.
Each event type has a listener interface with callback methods - ActionListener has actionPerformed, MouseListener has five methods.
Listeners must be registered using addXXXListener methods - without registration, events will not be received or processed.
g.text: For a listener to receive events, it must be registered with the source.
Use addXXXListener(XXXListener l) methods to register.

g.data-narration: Three ways to implement listeners: Anonymous inner classes for quick handlers, named inner classes for reuse, and container implementing interface.
Anonymous inner classes keep listener code directly where the component is set up, ideal for simple handlers.
Adapter classes provide empty implementations of listener methods, so you only override what you need instead of all methods.
g.text: Listener interfaces like MouseListener have 5 methods - implementing all is cumbersome.
Adapter Classes (MouseAdapter, WindowAdapter) provide empty implementations.
public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) { /* Only what you need */ }

g.data-narration: Lambda expressions simplify single-method listeners in Java 8+, reducing boilerplate code significantly.
g.text: For Single Abstract Method (SAM) interfaces like ActionListener:
Lambda expressions are concise, modern, and preferred for simple handlers.

g.data-narration: This calculator example demonstrates container implementing ActionListener with multiple button handling.
g.text: Frame implements ActionListener, uses e.getSource() to identify which button was clicked.
public class Calculator extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

g.data-narration: Key points: Delegation model separates source from listener, registration is essential, adapters simplify multi-method interfaces.
g.text: Source to Event Object to Listener
e to handle(e) for SAM interfaces

g.data-narration: Remember: registration is essential, adapters save writing empty methods, use e.getSource() for multiple handlers, and use EDT for Swing.
g.text: Listener will not work without registration. Use e.getSource() for multiple components.
Adapter classes avoid empty method stubs. Always use SwingUtilities.invokeLater() for EDT.
